title: How to Start Touch Typing
description: Touch typing is one of the highest ROI skills you can learn. It's not about speed — it's about freeing your brain from thinking about the keyboard.

steps.title: Learn the home row — it's your anchor
steps.instruction: Place your left fingers on A S D F and right fingers on J K L ;. Your index fingers rest on F and J — feel the raised bumps. You always return here between keystrokes. This is the foundation of everything.
steps.tip: Most keyboards have tactile bumps on F and J. Find them now.

media.caption: Touch typing fundamentals and finger placement

steps.title: Assign each finger its keys
steps.instruction: Left pinky: Q A Z. Left ring: W S X. Left middle: E D C. Left index: R F V T G B. Right index: Y H N U J M. Right middle: I K ,. Right ring: O L .. Right pinky: P ; /. Memorise this map — it's the whole system.
steps.tip: Print a keyboard layout diagram and keep it next to you.

steps.title: Do NOT look at the keyboard
steps.instruction: Cover your hands with a cloth, turn your keyboard upside down, or use a blank keyboard. Looking is a crutch that prevents muscle memory from forming. The discomfort of not looking is exactly where the learning happens.
steps.tip: The first day will feel agonizingly slow. That's normal — you're rewiring.

steps.title: Start with free tools: Keybr or Monkeytype
steps.instruction: Go to keybr.com. It starts with just 4 keys and adds more as your accuracy improves. This graduated approach is scientifically better than practicing the whole keyboard at once.
steps.tip: Keybr for building the habit; Monkeytype for testing speed once you have it.

steps.title: Accuracy over speed — always
steps.instruction: Slow down until you can type a key correctly 95% of the time before moving on. Speed is a byproduct of accuracy. Typing fast with errors means you're just reinforcing the wrong muscle patterns.
steps.tip: If you're making more than 1 mistake per 10 keystrokes, you're going too fast.

steps.title: Practice in short, focused bursts
steps.instruction: 15 minutes of focused practice beats 1 hour of distracted drilling. Set a timer for 15 minutes, practice with full attention, then stop. Two to three sessions per day is enough to see fast progress.
steps.tip: Your fingers build memory during rest, not just during practice.

steps.title: Use the correct finger every single time
steps.instruction: The moment you cheat and use the wrong finger 'just this once', you're creating a competing habit. If you catch yourself using the wrong finger, go back and retype the word correctly with the right one.
steps.tip: Consistency is everything in the early stages.

steps.title: Practice real words, not just random letters
steps.instruction: Random letter drills build key knowledge but not typing rhythm. After your first week, switch to typing real words and sentences — common English words follow predictable patterns your hands will memorize.
steps.tip: The 200 most common English words make up 50% of written text. Learn those first.

steps.title: Use touch typing for ALL your real work
steps.instruction: The fastest way to improve is to use touch typing in your actual work — emails, chats, documents. Every real-world sentence you type is practice. Switching back to hunt-and-peck for 'important' things defeats the purpose.
steps.tip: It will be slower at first. Accept the dip — it's temporary.

steps.title: Expect 60+ WPM within 3-6 months
steps.instruction: Most people reach conversational typing speed (40-60 WPM) within 6-8 weeks of daily practice. With dedicated effort, 80-100 WPM is achievable in 3-6 months. You're not just learning to type — you're upgrading a skill you'll use every day for the rest of your life.
steps.tip: Average hunt-and-peck is ~35 WPM. Touch typing unlocks 60-100 WPM. The ROI compounds forever.
